title: Bottleneck ResNets
concept: Bottleneck ResNets reuse Unit 100's 1x1 shrink -> 3x3 work -> 1x1 expand pattern inside residual blocks, letting very deep networks stay computationally reasonable.

exercises.prompt: Callback to Unit 100: Why does a 1x1 squeeze before a 3x3 convolution save computation?

options.text: Because the expensive 3x3 runs on fewer channels
Because 1x1 automatically doubles the image size
Because squeezing deletes the need for all later layers

exercises.explain: Right — bottleneck ResNets borrow that exact trick and place it inside residual blocks.

cards.title: You already learned the key trick
cards.body: Bottleneck ResNets can sound like a brand-new invention, but the core move should feel familiar. Unit 100 already taught the pattern: shrink channels, do the expensive spatial work, then expand again. ResNet simply turned that idea into a deep residual building block.

cards.title: A bottleneck residual block
cards.body: In a bottleneck ResNet block, the shortcut path carries the input forward while the main path typically goes 1x1 reduce -> 3x3 process -> 1x1 expand. The middle 3x3 is where the local spatial mixing happens, but it operates on a compressed channel width instead of the full width.

cards.title: Pack, travel, unpack
cards.body: Imagine sending equipment to another city. Shipping the full warehouse is expensive, so you pack only the essential gear into a smaller box, move it through the costly transport step, then unpack into a full workstation on arrival. Bottleneck blocks do the same with channels.

cards.title: A typical channel story
cards.body: A bottleneck block might take 256 channels in, squeeze to 64 with a 1x1, run the spatial 3x3 at 64 channels, then expand back to 256. The skip path can still meet that output shape, so the residual addition remains clean.

cards.title: The 1x1 layers are not cosmetic
cards.body: It is easy to think the middle 3x3 is the 'real' block and the surrounding 1x1 layers are just paperwork. In practice, those 1x1 layers are exactly what make deep bottleneck ResNets feasible: they control width, compute cost, and shape compatibility.

cards.title: Where the savings happen
cards.body: The expensive part stays, but it runs in a narrower channel space.

left.text:
Run every 3x3 at the full channel count
Depth gets expensive quickly

right.text:
Use 1x1 -> 3x3 -> 1x1
Keep the costly 3x3 in a narrower space

exercises.prompt: What is the main architectural reason bottleneck blocks help very deep ResNets?

options.text: They keep the expensive spatial convolution in a reduced channel space
They remove residual shortcuts so the network is simpler
They replace every 3x3 convolution with a pooling layer
They force all feature maps to stay at 3 channels

exercises.explain: Exactly — the bottleneck pattern is really a compute-management trick inside a residual block.

exercises.prompt: True or false: the middle 3x3 convolution in a bottleneck block usually runs after channels have been squeezed down.
exercises.explain: True — that squeezed middle is the whole reason the pattern saves cost.

exercises.sentence: Inside a bottleneck ResNet block, the usual order is shrink -> ___ -> expand.
exercises.explain: Right — the 3x3 is still the spatial workhorse, just surrounded by channel-control layers.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly connects Unit 100 to bottleneck ResNets?

options.text: The same squeeze-work-expand pattern from 1x1 bottlenecks becomes the internal recipe of deeper residual blocks
Unit 100's 1x1 convolutions were unrelated and never appear in later named CNNs
Bottleneck ResNets use 1x1 only for the skip path and never on the main path

exercises.explain: Yes — this is a direct callback, not a coincidence.

exercises.prompt: Why is the phrase 'bottleneck ResNet' meaningful instead of just being another name for any deep ResNet?

options.text: Because it tells you the residual block itself uses the squeeze -> 3x3 -> expand pattern to make deep stacking efficient
Because it means the network has no 3x3 convolutions anywhere
Because it means the shortcut path is deleted to save memory
Because it refers only to the dataset size, not the architecture

exercises.explain: Exactly. 'Bottleneck' is architectural shorthand for how the residual block manages compute. That is why models like ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 can go much deeper without naively paying full-width cost at every stage. Next, we'll read the family names themselves more clearly: 18, 34, 50, 101.
